condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I began by pulling the Metaculus question details for question 43568, confirming the resolution criteria: the question resolves YES if Chris Nanos is the sitting Sheriff of Pima County, Arizona at 23:59 MST on May 31, 2026. A temporary delegation of duties does not resolve NO, but a formal suspension (e.g., by court order) does.

I then ran two parallel research queries:
1. **Current status of Chris Nanos** — confirming he was publicly active as sheriff as recently as **May 12, 2026**, giving press conferences on the Nancy Guthrie investigation across KOLD News, The Hill, Newsweek, IBTimes, and Hello Magazine. No evidence of resignation or removal was found.
2. **Arizona law on sheriff removal** — documenting the three primary mechanisms: judicial removal (ARS §§38-291 through 38-296), recall election (Arizona Constitution Article 12 + ARS Title 19), and automatic forfeiture upon felony conviction. Key timelines: recall requires **120 days** of signature collection plus an election (6–9 months total), judicial removal requires filing in Superior Court with 10–20 day answer period then summary trial, and the governor has **no** unilateral removal power.

## Key Evidence

- **Board of Supervisors vote (May 12–13, 2026)**: Multiple sources (KOLD, AZ Luminaria, KGUN9, Fox News, ktar.com) confirmed the Board voted **not to remove** Nanos. Supervisor Christy's motion to vacate failed without a second. The Board voted **4-0** (one abstention) to refer perjury allegations to the Arizona AG instead.
- **Recall procedurally blocked**: Under Arizona law, a recall requires 120 days of signature gathering plus 20–30 days for an election — structurally impossible before May 31 with only 9 days remaining.
- **AG perjury referral**: Made only ~10 days before the forecast date. Criminal investigations and prosecutions take months to years. No charges or court action expected in 9 days.
- **FBI investigation**: Ongoing probe into whether Nanos delayed FBI involvement in the Guthrie case. No public indication of imminent charges or arrest.
- **Deputies' union no-confidence vote**: **241 voted no confidence / resign** vs. 65 abstained (March 24, 2026). Significant political pressure but no legal force.
- **No resignation signals**: Nanos continued giving media updates and actively defending his position through at least May 12. His lawyer disputed characterizations of his El Paso record.

I ran four web searches to look for any very recent news (May 21–22) about resignation, removal, or new legal action. All results returned the same May 12–13 Board vote coverage — no new developments.

## Conclusion

I concluded at **96% YES** — Nanos will still be sheriff on May 31, 2026. The anchor was the base rate for an elected sheriff remaining in office over a 9-day period (~99%+), adjusted down ~3 percentage points to account for the extraordinary political pressure (Board referral, perjury allegations, FBI probe, no-confidence vote). Every formal removal mechanism is either exhausted (Board vote failed), procedurally impossible (recall), or too early-stage to produce results in 9 days (AG referral, FBI investigation). The only realistic NO scenarios are voluntary resignation (~2–3%), health emergency (~0.1%), or surprise rapid legal action (~1%), totaling roughly 4% residual risk.

capability_gaps: No tool to directly fetch and render the pimasheriff.org website, which is the primary resolution source
subtle_bugs: Research tool cached data through ~May 19 but returned this as 'fresh' state, potentially giving false confidence about recency when the question has a May 31 resolution with a May 22 forecast date

classification.approach: Systematic enumeration of all removal mechanisms against the 9-day timeline, anchored on base rate of incumbency stability, adjusted for extraordinary political pressure.
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether Nanos might voluntarily resign under extraordinary political pressure despite showing no public signs of doing so.

risk_flags.evidence: The resolution criteria explicitly state 'The primary resolution source is the Pima County Sheriff's Department official website (https://www.pimasheriff.org/)' and a search result even returned Nanos's resume PDF from that site, but the agent never fetched the website directly to confirm current status.

The research tool's cached data extended only through approximately May 19, 2026, and the most recent confirmed Nanos activity was May 12 (KOLD press conference). Web searches for May 21-22 returned no results from those dates. There is a 3-day gap where a development could have occurred undetected.

strengths: Systematically enumerated every removal mechanism (Board vote, recall, judicial removal, gubernatorial power, automatic forfeiture) and assessed each one's feasibility within the 9-day window — this is exactly the right analytical framework for this question.
Correctly identified the Board of Supervisors vote on May 12-13 as the critical recent event and confirmed it from multiple independent news sources (KOLD, AZ Luminaria, KGUN9, Fox News, ktar.com).

Researched Arizona legal procedures in depth (ARS §§38-291 through 38-296, Title 19 recall provisions) to establish that formal removal and recall are procedurally blocked within the timeframe — not just asserted but substantiated with specific statutes and timelines.
Ran a targeted search for very recent news (May 21-22) to check for surprise developments, finding none — good practice for a short-horizon question.

Explicitly acknowledged factor correlation in the reflection step ('factor sum implies 99.9% but factors are somewhat redundant') and adjusted the final probability accordingly rather than mechanically summing correlated factors.

Premortem counterargument was substantive — constructed a genuine alternative scenario involving negotiated resignation, lawyer advice about legal exposure, and FBI moving quickly, rather than a token exercise.
The 96% estimate is well-calibrated: high enough to reflect the overwhelming status-quo advantage, but not so high as to ignore the genuine extraordinary pressures Nanos faces.
weaknesses: Did not attempt to check prediction markets (Polymarket, Kalshi, Manifold) for this question — while likely no liquid market exists for a county sheriff question, the check would have been fast and worthwhile.

Research tool data was cached through approximately May 19, and the agent acknowledged this limitation but didn't find a way to get fresher data — the web searches returned results mostly from May 12-13 with nothing from May 19-22.
Did not check the primary resolution source (pimasheriff.org) directly, despite it being explicitly listed in the resolution criteria and appearing in search results as still listing Nanos.

The fine print about formal suspension resolving NO was only implicitly addressed through AG timeline and tail risk factors — could have been more explicitly analyzed as a distinct scenario (e.g., someone filing a judicial removal petition under ARS §38-292 and getting a discretionary suspension order under §38-295).
actionable_improvements: Check the primary resolution source (pimasheriff.org) directly when it is explicitly named in the resolution criteria — even a web fetch confirming the site still lists Nanos would strengthen the forecast.

Attempt to check prediction markets even for niche political questions — a quick search on Polymarket/Manifold for 'Nanos' or 'Pima County Sheriff' takes seconds and could reveal market-implied probabilities.

More explicitly analyze the fine-print suspension scenario as a distinct path: could anyone file a judicial removal petition under ARS §38-292 and get a discretionary suspension order under §38-295 within 9 days? The agent's Arizona law research already contained this information.
For short-horizon questions (9 days), prioritize the freshest possible data sources — consider searching for news from the last 24-48 hours specifically rather than broad date-range searches.
